title.zh: 大荒诸经中的至上天帝
title.en: The High God of the Great Wilderness Classics

summary.zh: 只出现在大荒四经与海内经的天帝：妻羲和生十日，妻常羲生十二月，子孙发明了舟、车、琴瑟、歌舞与百工技巧。
summary.en: A high god found only in the Great Wilderness and final chapters: his wife Xihe bore the ten suns, Changxi the twelve moons, and his descendants invented boats, carts, zithers, song and craft.

description.zh:
帝俊之名不见于五藏山经，也不见于先秦其他传世典籍，却在大荒四经与海内经里出现十余次，是这一层文本中辈分最高的天帝。他的三位妻子各自惊人：羲和在东南海外的甘渊生下十个太阳，常羲在大荒之西生下十二个月亮，娥皇生下一首三身的三身之国。大荒东经还说有五采之鸟自歌自舞，「惟帝俊下友」，帝在下界的两座祭坛正由这些彩鸟看管。

海内经把上古发明谱系几乎全挂在他名下：帝俊生禺号，禺号的后代番禺开始造舟，再传到吉光，开始用木头造车；晏龙开始制作琴和瑟；帝俊的八个儿子开始了歌与舞；三身的后代义均就是巧倕，为下界民众发明了各种工巧。十日为害时，也是帝俊赐给羿彤弓素矰，命他去救助下方国土的百般艰难。

这样一位大神在经外文献中几乎无影无踪，成为经学史上的悬案。郭璞注以「俊」为「舜」的假借；1917年王国维据甲骨卜辞考出殷人祭祀的「高祖夒」，郭沫若进而主张夒即帝俊、亦即文献中的帝喾。帝俊、帝喾、舜三者同源分化，是现代神话学的通行假说，也使帝俊被视为商人至上神在《山海经》中留下的孤本身影。
description.en:
The name Di Jun appears nowhere in the five books of mountain itineraries and in no other transmitted pre-Qin text, yet he surfaces more than a dozen times across the four Great Wilderness classics and the final chapter, where he outranks every other god. His three wives are each remarkable: Xihe bore the ten suns and bathed them in the Sweet Gulf beyond the southeastern sea; Changxi bore the twelve moons in the western wilderness; Ehuang bore the one-headed, three-bodied people of the Sanshen country. Five-colored birds that sing and dance for themselves are called 'the friends of Di Jun below', keeping his two altars on earth.

The final chapter hangs the genealogy of invention on him: his descendant Fanyu first built boats and Jiguang first built wooden carts; Yanlong first made the qin and se zithers; his eight sons began song and dance; and Yijun, of the three-bodied line, invented the hundred crafts for the people below. When the ten suns brought calamity, it was Di Jun who gave the archer Yi a vermilion bow and plain arrows and sent him down to relieve the lands beneath.

Why so great a god vanished from all other early literature became a classic puzzle. The commentator Guo Pu read 'Jun' as a loan for 'Shun'; in 1917 Wang Guowei identified the 'High Ancestor Nao' worshipped in Shang oracle-bone inscriptions, and Guo Moruo argued this figure was Di Jun himself, identical with Di Ku of the transmitted histories. The three-way identification remains the standard hypothesis: Di Jun as the supreme god of the Shang, surviving only in this book.

value.zh: 羲和（生十日）、常羲（生十二月）、娥皇（生三身之国）
value.en: Xihe (ten suns), Changxi (twelve moons), Ehuang (the Three-Bodied country)

value.zh: 仅见于大荒四经与海内经，传世先秦他书无载
value.en: Only in the Great Wilderness classics and the final chapter; absent from other pre-Qin texts

value.zh: 甲骨文高祖夒，或即帝喾、舜之原型
value.en: The Shang 'High Ancestor Nao' of oracle bones; prototype of Di Ku and Shun

sources.zh: 《山海经·大荒东经》《大荒南经》《大荒西经》《海内经》
sources.en: Classic of Mountains and Seas, Great Wilderness classics and final chapter
Guo Pu's commentary on the Classic of Mountains and Seas

sources.zh: 王国维《殷卜辞中所见先公先王考》（1917）
sources.en: Wang Guowei, 'The Pre-dynastic Lords and Kings of Shang Seen in Yin Oracle Inscriptions' (1917)

volume.en: Classic of the Great Wilderness: East
